1: 'The word of the LORD came to me' — the oracular formula opens chapter 2's extended covenant lawsuit, which continues through chapter 6 (the 'foe from the north' section). The transition from the call narrative (Jeremiah 1) to the first major address follows the standard prophetic book structure: authorization (Jeremiah 1) precedes proclamation (Jeremiah 2ff.).
2: 'I remember the devotion of your youth, the love of your bridal days, how you followed me through the wilderness' — YHWH's opening move in the lawsuit is nostalgic — he recalls the covenant's honeymoon period. The 'devotion of your youth' is ḥesed nĕʿûrayik — Israel's own ḥesed (covenant loyalty) during the wilderness period. This is the inverse of YHWH's ḥesed toward Israel; here Israel reciprocated. The wilderness period is idealized throughout the prophets (Hosea 2:14-15; Ezekiel 20:10-13; Deuteronomy 32:10-11) as a time of exclusive dependence on YHWH before the land and its Canaanite temptations intervened.
3: 'Israel was holy to the LORD, the firstfruits of his harvest; all who consumed them were held guilty' — Israel as YHWH's qōdeš (holy/set-apart) and rēʾšît tĕbûʾātōh (firstfruits of his harvest) applies the Levitical firstfruits category (Exodus 23:19; Leviticus 23:10) to the nation itself. The firstfruits were YHWH's portion — untouchable by others; consuming them incurred guilt. This establishes the theological basis for the judgment oracles: the nations who attacked Israel attacked something consecrated to YHWH, and the consequences were divinely mandated. The passage simultaneously vindicates the covenant relationship and condemns Israel's apostasy.
4: 'Hear the word of the LORD, O house of Jacob, all the families of Israel' — the lawsuit begins with the formal summons to hear, a standard covenant-lawsuit opening (cf. Isaiah 1:2: 'Hear, O heavens! Listen, O earth!'; Micah 6:1-2: 'Hear what the LORD says'). 'All the families of Israel' (kol-mišpĕḥôt bêt yiśrāʾēl) addresses the tribal structure of the covenant people — the lawsuit is not against individuals but against the covenant community as a corporate entity. The 'house of Jacob' phrasing connects to the ancestral covenant and situates the dispute within the long covenant history.
5: 'What fault did your fathers find in me, that they went so far from me, following worthless idols and becoming worthless themselves?' — the covenant lawsuit's first argument: YHWH challenges the prosecution's case by demanding evidence of divine wrongdoing. The gods Israel followed are called hahebel (the vanity/breath/vapor) — the same word that governs Ecclesiastes ('vanity of vanities'). The word-play is incisive: those who follow breath-idols (hebel) become vapor-people (wayehbāl) themselves — the idol shapes its worshipper. The argument is: there was no legitimate reason for the defection; the gods Israel chose are ontologically inferior to the covenant partner they abandoned.
6: 'They did not ask, Where is the LORD who brought us up out of Egypt, who led us through the wilderness?' — the wilderness is cited as the test-case of divine reliability: YHWH led Israel through 'a land of deserts and ravines, through a land of drought and deep darkness.' The rhetorical point is that Israel already has empirical evidence of YHWH's provision in the most extreme conditions imaginable. Not asking 'Where is the LORD?' represents willful theological amnesia — a failure to trace the present back to the proven historical pattern of divine faithfulness.
7: 'I brought you into a land of plenty, to enjoy its fruit and abundance. But when you entered, you defiled my land and made my heritage loathsome' — the land-gift and land-defilement are placed in immediate sequence without transitional explanation: the gift was answered with defilement. 'My land' (ʾarṣî) and 'my heritage' (naḥălātî) assert divine ownership of Canaan that pre-exists the covenant: YHWH does not give Israel land that becomes theirs; he gives them tenancy rights in land that remains his. The defilement (timmettem) is the ritual-purity term — Canaanite cult practices made the land unclean in the same way they made people unclean.
8: 'The priests did not ask, Where is the LORD? Those who deal with the law did not know me; the rulers transgressed against me; the prophets prophesied by Baal' — the comprehensive leadership indictment: all four pillars of Israelite society have failed. Priests (liturgical custodians), law-teachers/scribes (tōpĕśê hattôrāh), rulers (rōʿîm, shepherds/kings), and prophets — each group identified by their function and indicted for its failure. The prophets prophesying by Baal is a specific historical reference: the Baal cult had a prophetic dimension (450 prophets of Baal: 1 Kings 18:19), and false prophets within Israel had adopted Baal-prophetic practices.
9: 'Therefore I will bring charges against you again, declares the LORD, and against your grandchildren will I bring charges' — the lawsuit (rîb) will continue generationally. The covenant-curse framework of Deuteronomy 5:9 (YHWH 'visiting the iniquity of the fathers upon the children to the third and fourth generation') applies here not merely as punishment but as prosecution: the transgenerational accountability of the covenant makes each generation responsible both for its own sin and for the legacy of its predecessors.
10: 'Cross to the shores of Cyprus and look; send to Kedar and examine carefully' — Cyprus (Kittim, the western Mediterranean) and Kedar (northern Arabia, the east) form a geographic merism covering the known world's extremes. Jeremiah invites his audience to conduct a comparative religion survey: examine the ends of the earth for evidence of any nation abandoning its gods. The argument is from the bizarre: even pagan nations with manifestly inferior, non-existent gods maintain covenant loyalty to them. Israel's defection from the living God is more than religious failure — it is a sociological anomaly.
11: 'Has any nation changed its gods, even though they are not real gods? But my people have exchanged their glory for things that are worthless' — the irony is acute: nations who worship genuinely inferior deities stay loyal to them; Israel, who has the genuinely superior covenant partner, has traded up for inferiors. 'My glory' (kĕbôdô, his glory — the LXX reads 'their glory') refers to YHWH himself as the source of Israel's honor and status among nations. To exchange him for Baals is not merely a religious mistake but a catastrophic status-inversion: trading patron-position with the creator for vassalage to powerless regional spirits.
12: 'Be appalled at this, O heavens; shudder with great horror, declares the LORD' — the heavens are called as shocked witnesses, an expansion of the cosmic-witness role in the covenant lawsuit. The imperative to the heavens to be appalled (šōmmû haššāmayim) is stronger than the Deuteronomic witness formula (Deuteronomy 32:1: 'Listen, O heavens') — here the heavens are not merely called to testify but to recoil in horror. The prophetic imagination treats the cosmos as morally responsive: creation itself is outraged by covenant violation.
13: 'My people have committed two evils: they have abandoned me, the spring of living water, and dug their own cisterns — cracked cisterns that cannot hold water' — the water-source metaphor is one of the most powerful in Jeremiah. YHWH as mĕqôr mayim ḥayyîm (spring of living water) — an active, flowing, fresh source — is set against man-made bōrôt (cisterns), and specifically cracked ones. In Palestine's semi-arid climate, the cistern was the alternative to the spring: a rock-hewn reservoir that collected and held rainwater. A cracked cistern cannot hold what it collects — it is not even a reliable artificial substitute for the natural spring. The two failures are progressive: first abandoning the real, then trusting the inadequate.
14: 'Is Israel a slave? Was he born into slavery? Then why has he become plunder?' — the rhetorical questions probe the cause of Israel's subjugation. The assumed answer is 'no' — Israel is not a natural slave class; the covenant relationship with YHWH granted them the status of YHWH's firstborn son (Exodus 4:22). The rhetorical logic: if Israel is not naturally enslaved, something must have caused the current status of being plundered by enemies. The answer will come in Jeremiah 2:17: they brought it on themselves. The 'plunder' (lĕbaz) anticipates the Assyrian and Babylonian deportations already underway for the northern kingdom and coming for Judah.
15: 'Young lions have roared against him and growled; they have laid his land waste' — the young lions (kĕpîrîm) are a standard ANE metaphor for devastating military invaders (Nahum 2:11-12; Isaiah 5:29; Psalms 34:10 uses the same term for powerful predators). The devastation description — cities burned, land emptied — corresponds to the Assyrian campaign against the Northern Kingdom (734-722 BCE) and to the Babylonian campaigns against Judah (605-586 BCE). Jeremiah uses the image as present-tense warning: this is happening now to Israel, and it will happen to Judah unless the trajectory changes.
16: 'The people of Memphis and Tahpanhes have shaved the top of your head' — Memphis (Nōp) and Tahpanhes (Taḥpanḥēs, the modern Tell Defenna in the Nile Delta) are Egyptian cities, representing Egypt as Judah's unreliable political ally. 'Shaving the top of the head' is a humiliation idiom — the Egyptians have degraded rather than protected Judah. The critique points to Josiah's alliance politics: his death at Megiddo (609 BCE) came while trying to block Pharaoh Necho's march to Assyria, suggesting pro-Egyptian factionalism in the Judean court that Jeremiah consistently opposed. Egypt will fail Judah at the critical moment.
17: 'Have you not brought this on yourself by forsaking the LORD your God when he led you on the way?' — the self-causation of disaster is the covenant's built-in logic: the curses of Deuteronomy 28 are not arbitrary divine punishment but the natural consequence of choosing covenant violation. 'On the way' (bĕdērek) may refer to the Exodus journey — the moment when YHWH demonstrated faithful guidance (cf. Jeremiah 2:6's wilderness reference) and Israel responded with faithlessness. The wilderness testing-and-failure pattern (Exod 16-17; Num 11-21) is the historical paradigm for the ongoing pattern Jeremiah is diagnosing.
18: 'And now why go down to Egypt to drink from the waters of the Nile? Or why go to Assyria to drink from the Euphrates?' — Egypt (Nile) and Assyria (Euphrates) represent the two great imperial powers between which Judah's court played its political game. The 'drinking water' metaphor for political alliance reflects the ANE patron-client system: a small state 'drinking' from a great power's river means depending on its patronage, military support, and political protection. Jeremiah consistently opposed both Egyptian and Babylonian alliance as alternatives to covenant trust in YHWH — the anti-treaty theology runs through his ministry (Jeremiah 27:5-8).
19: 'Your own wickedness will punish you, and your turning away will rebuke you' — tĕyassĕrĕkā rāʿātēk: the punishment is internal to the sin, not externally applied as separate divine action. This is the covenant's self-executing logic: abandoning the spring of living water creates its own punishment (thirst); turning from the covenant partner who provides security creates vulnerability to the enemies you sought protection from. 'Know and see that it is evil and bitter to forsake the LORD' — the daʿ wĕrĕʾî (know and see) is an invitation to empirical investigation: look at what has happened, and understand the causality.
20: 'For long ago I broke your yoke and tore off your chains. But you said, “I will not serve!” And on every high hill and under every spreading tree you gave yourself like a prostitute' — the yoke-and-chains broken are the Exodus liberation: YHWH's founding act was precisely to free Israel from forced service to Pharaoh. Israel's response was to declare 'I will not serve!' — the same verb ʿābad (serve) used for both Egyptian slavery and covenant worship. The irony: freed from servitude, Israel voluntarily enslaves itself again to Baal at every high hill and spreading tree. The high places (bāmôt) and sacred trees (ʾayyil raʿănan) were the standard Canaanite cult-installation locations.
21: 'I had planted you as a choice vine, of pure and wholesome seed. How then have you turned against me into the wild shoots of a foreign vine?' — the choice-vine (śōrēq, the finest variety of grape) metaphor pictures YHWH as the careful vinedresser who planted Israel with genetic precision. The transformation into a gepen nŏkrîyyāh (foreign vine, degenerate shoots) represents a categorical degradation — not merely poor performance but a change in essential nature. Isaiah 5:1-7 develops the same vine-owner metaphor at length; Jesus's 'I am the true vine' (John 15:1) takes up the same imagery with the claim to be what Israel failed to be.
22: 'Even if you scrubbed yourself with lye and used much soap, the stain of your guilt would still be before me' — the stain (nikĕtām ʿăwōnēk) that resists all ritual purification agents is a picture of guilt that has become structural rather than transactional. Natron-lye (bōrit) and soap (neter) were the standard ANE cleaning agents; the point is that the most powerful physical purification methods cannot reach this depth of defilement. Psalms 51:7 ('Wash me, and I shall be whiter than snow') implicitly acknowledges the same problem — only divine action can accomplish what human purification cannot.
23: 'How can you say, “I am not defiled, I have not chased after the Baals”? Look at your tracks in the valley' — the valley (gĕyʾ) probably refers to the Valley of Ben Hinnom (Topheth) south of Jerusalem, where child sacrifice to Molech was practiced (Jeremiah 7:31; Jeremiah 19:5-6; 2 Kings 23:10). The 'tracks' (dĕrākayik baggāyʾ) are the evidence that refutes the denial: the physical places of cultic activity are visible. The 'swift young camel' simile pictures frantic, undirected motion — the compulsive energy of religious syncretism that lacks the purposeful direction of covenant loyalty.
24: 'Like a wild donkey at home in the desert, sniffing the wind in her craving — who can hold her back when she is in heat?' — the wild-donkey-in-heat is an extended metaphor for the uncontrollable appetite of Israel's idolatry. The simile is deliberately crude: the most ungovernable animal drive (a female animal in estrus) is used to picture Israel's compulsive attraction to foreign cults. The point is that no human intervention can restrain it — 'all who look for her need not tire themselves; they will find her in her mating season.' Israel's unfaithfulness is not a momentary lapse but a structural addiction.
25: 'Keep your feet from going bare and your throat from being dry. But you said, “It is hopeless! I love other gods, and after them I will go”' — the warning about bare feet and a dry throat is metaphorical: running after lovers leads to exhaustion. YHWH's counsel is to stop the running before it leads to ruin. But the response ('It is hopeless!': nôʾāš, despair) is a declaration of covenant abandonment — not ignorance or confusion, but a deliberate choice to declare the covenant unworkable and pursue the alternative. This is the moral low point of the chapter: the explicit, knowing decision to abandon YHWH for other gods.
26: 'As a thief is put to shame when caught, so the house of Israel will be shamed — they, their kings, their officials, their priests, and their prophets' — the shame-when-caught image applies to the whole leadership hierarchy listed in the same order as Jeremiah 2:8. The shame (bōšet) is a comprehensive covenant-consequence: honor was the expected outcome of covenant faithfulness (Deuteronomy 26:19: YHWH will make Israel 'high above all nations'); shame is the consequence of its reversal. The list king-officials-priests-prophets is the same leadership structure that will fail during the siege of Jerusalem (Lamentations 4:12-16).
27: 'They say to a tree, “You are my father,” and to a stone, “You gave me birth”' — the tree-and-stone worship targets the Asherah cult and the standing stones (maṣṣēbāh) of Canaanite religion. The Asherah was a wooden pole or stylized tree associated with the goddess Asherah, consort of El; 'you are my father' addressed to the tree represents a fundamental reordering of the divine family: YHWH (the true Father: Isaiah 63:16; Jeremiah 64:8) is displaced by a fertility goddess's wooden symbol. 'They have turned their backs to me and not their faces. Yet in the time of their trouble they will cry, Rise up and save us!' — the YHWH-rejection is reversed only in crisis, reflecting the complaint-and-rescue pattern of the Judges cycles.
28: 'But where are the gods you made for yourself? Let them rise up, if they can rescue you in the time of your trouble — for you have as many gods as you have cities, O Judah' — the challenge-to-the-idol motif (cf. Isaiah 41:21-24; Jeremiah 44:9-20) invites empirical evaluation: if the Baals are real, let them act. The 'as many gods as cities' formula (kĕmispar ʿārêk hāyû ʾĕlōhêkā) points to the local Baal-shrines at each town's high place — each city had its local Baal, and each Baal-shrine represented a separate defection from the covenant. The multiplication of worship sites was both the symptom and the intensification of the apostasy.
29: 'Why do you bring a complaint against me? You have all rebelled against me, declares the LORD' — the covenant lawsuit has an unexpected reversal: Israel might bring a counter-complaint that YHWH's punishment was excessive, but YHWH preempts it by pointing out that universal rebellion (kullĕkem pĕšaʿtem bî) disqualifies any counter-claim. The rîb (lawsuit) runs in one direction only when the evidence of violation is total. The legal frame makes Israel's potential objection frivolous rather than substantive.
30: 'I have struck your people in vain; they accepted no correction. Your own sword has consumed your prophets like a ravaging lion' — the divine discipline (hikkîtî ʾet-bĕnêkem lĕšāwĕʾ) has already occurred and failed. The history of discipline without repentance reaches its logical conclusion: when correction produces no correction, escalated consequences follow. 'Your own sword has consumed your prophets like a ravaging lion' refers to the persecution of true prophets by the establishment — a pattern evidenced in the murder of the prophet Uriah (Jeremiah 26:20-23) and the consistent opposition to Jeremiah himself.
31: 'O generation, take heed of the word of the LORD: Have I been a desert to Israel or a land of thick darkness? Why do my people say, “We are free; we will come to you no more”?' — YHWH as defendant: the rhetorical question invites the people to evaluate whether the covenant relationship has been oppressive or beneficial. A desert (midbār) and thick darkness (maḥšāk) are the conditions of danger and divine absence; YHWH asks whether his governance has been experienced as that. 'We are free' (rādnû, we have roamed free) is the people's claim that covenant obligation was servitude — a claim YHWH challenges by pointing to the actual benefits of the relationship.
32: 'Does a young woman forget her jewelry, or a bride her wedding attire? Yet my people have forgotten me — for more days than can be counted' — the comparison to a bride's accessories (ʿădî, ornaments; kišrōtêhā, wedding sash or bridal attire) illustrates the improbability of Israel's forgetfulness. A bride's ornaments are her most prized possessions, never forgotten even momentarily. Israel's forgetting of YHWH across countless days is thus more extreme than the most unthinkable human forgetfulness. The bride imagery also anticipates the marriage metaphor of chapter 3 and the larger prophetic tradition of YHWH as Israel's husband (Hos 2; Ezek 16).
33: 'How skilled you are at pursuing love; you have become expert enough to teach the wicked your ways' — the ironic compliment: Israel has become so proficient at idolatrous religious practice that it has exceeded the Canaanites ('the wicked') in expertise. The 'pursuing love' is baqēš ʾahăbāh — seeking lovers, the standard idiom for religious prostitution at cult shrines. The reversal is complete: Israel, called to teach the nations covenantal wisdom (Deuteronomy 4:6-8: 'this is your wisdom in the sight of the peoples'), has become a master-teacher in the practice of covenant violation.
34: 'On your garments is found the lifeblood of innocent poor people — you did not catch them breaking in; it is on all these' — the blood of the innocent poor is visible evidence of social injustice that Jeremiah adds to the religious indictment. Unlike a thief caught breaking in (Exodus 22:2: not guilty for killing a burglar), the blood on Israel's garments comes from unsuspecting innocents — premeditated violence against the vulnerable. Amos (Jeremiah 2:6-8; Jeremiah 5:11-12) and Isaiah (Jeremiah 1:21-23; Jeremiah 3:14-15) develop the same connection between idolatry and social injustice: false religion and exploitation of the poor are two aspects of the same covenant failure.
35: 'Yet you say, “I am innocent; surely his anger has turned from me.” But I will bring judgment against you because you say, “I have not sinned”' — the self-exculpation (niqqêtî, I am clean/innocent) while the blood of the poor is visible on the garments is the most extreme moral blindness in the chapter. The denial of sin (lōʾ ḥāṭāʾtî) is not ignorance but a deliberate refusal to acknowledge guilt. In the covenant framework, this refusal itself becomes grounds for judgment — it represents the hardening of the heart that makes repentance structurally impossible without divine intervention (the heart-circumcision of Deuteronomy 30:6).
36: 'Why do you rush about so much, changing course? You will be disappointed by Egypt just as you were disappointed by Assyria' — the political instability — rushing between Egypt and Assyria as alternative patrons — mirrors the religious instability of chasing different Baals. Both Jeremiah (Jeremiah 37:5-10) and the broader DH tradition (2 Kings 18-19: Hezekiah's temptation to rely on Egypt) treat pro-Egyptian policy as covenant unfaithfulness: it demonstrates that the court trusts imperial military power rather than YHWH's provision. Egypt's failure at Carchemish (605 BCE, Jeremiah 46:2) and at the siege of Jerusalem (Jeremiah 37:7-8) will fulfill this prediction.
37: 'You will leave Egypt too with your hands on your head in defeat, for the LORD has rejected the ones you are trusting, and you will not succeed through them' — hands on the head was the posture of mourning and defeat (2 Samuel 13:19; cf. Lamentations 2:10). The prediction is historically fulfilled in Jeremiah 37:5-8: Pharaoh's army came out of Egypt and withdrew when faced by the Babylonians, leaving Jerusalem without the Egyptian relief force. The 'LORD has rejected' (mēʾas Yhwh) the Egyptian alliance means that YHWH's covenantal opposition to the substitution of Egyptian trust for covenant trust is active: the alliance is not merely ineffective but divinely opposed.
